metadata.title: Build a Lesson AI-Integration Audit with Claude

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. Build it with Claude — then take it apart.

Most 'AI-integrated' lessons are really 'AI-adjacent.' The AI is doing something, but not the thing that would actually change what students learn. A lesson where AI is central to everything is just a lesson where students watch an AI work. The audit reveals whether integration is purposeful or cosmetic.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: problem hook. 'AI-integrated vs AI-adjacent.' The distinction that the audit reveals.

beats.narration_text: Pipe the lesson plan to Claude. For each activity, classify the AI's role as: central — the AI performs the cognitive work the objective targets; peripheral — the AI supports but the student does the key thinking; or decorative — the AI is present but not meaningfully affecting learning toward the objective.

props.command: claude "Read this lesson plan and its learning objectives. For each activity, classify the AI's role as: central (AI performs the cognitive work the objective targets), peripheral (AI supports but student does the key thinking), or decorative (AI present but not affecting learning toward the objective). Provide one-sentence reasoning for each classification." < lesson_plan.md

beats.narration_text: Read the script. It reads the lesson plan markdown, sends it to Claude with the three-role classification instruction, and formats the output as a lesson map — each activity block labeled with its AI role and the reasoning for the classification cited against the specific learning objective.

props.code:
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

ROLES = ["central", "peripheral", "decorative"]

def audit_integration(lesson_file):
    lesson = Path(lesson_file).read_text()
    instruction = (
        "For each activity in this lesson plan, classify\n"
        "the AI's role as:\n"
        "central: AI does the objective's cognitive work\n"
        "peripheral: AI supports, student does key thinking\n"
        "decorative: AI present, not affecting the objective\n"
        "One-sentence reasoning per classification."
    )
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["claude", instruction],
        input=lesson, capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    print(result.stdout)

beats.narration_text: The lesson map appears. Four activities classified. Activity one — 'Ask Claude to explain the concept': central. The explanation is the objective's cognitive work; the student is receiving, not thinking. Activity two — 'Use Claude to check your claim': peripheral. The student formed the claim; Claude is checking the form. Activity three — 'Read Claude's summary before class': decorative. The lesson outcome is identical if Claude is removed. Activity four — 'Write a response to Claude's explanation': peripheral. The student is doing the evaluative work.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: four-activity lesson map. Each activity block color-coded: central (red), peripheral (green), decorative (gray). Reasoning cited against the learning objective for each.

beats.narration_text: The change: redesign the activity with the worst classification — the one classified as central when it shouldn't be — to shift the cognitive work back to the student. Then re-run the audit and show the classification change.

props.command: claude "Activity 1 is classified as central — the AI is doing the cognitive work the objective targets. Redesign this activity so the student performs the objective's cognitive work and Claude supports rather than substitutes. Then re-classify and show what changed." < activity_one.md

beats.narration_text: Redesigned activity one: instead of 'Ask Claude to explain the concept,' the student generates their own explanation first, then asks Claude to identify gaps or errors in their explanation. The cognitive work — concept explanation — is now done by the student. Claude's role shifts from central to peripheral. The classification changes from central to peripheral. The lesson outcome changes from 'students received an explanation' to 'students produced an explanation and evaluated it against Claude's.'
beats.visual_intent: Slate: before/after for activity one. Central → peripheral. What the student does in each version. The cognitive work shift made explicit.

beats.narration_text: 'AI-integrated' is not a quality metric. A lesson where AI is central to everything is just a lesson where students watch an AI work. The audit reveals whether integration is purposeful or cosmetic — and gives you the design language to change it.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: teardown summary. 'Central = watching AI work. Peripheral = AI serves the student's thinking. Decorative = AI adds nothing.' The three roles as a quality vocabulary.

beats.narration_text: Next: Build an Instructional Brief Generator — guide an instructor through an eight-field brief and show how the generated prompt changes between a minimal brief and a complete one.
Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. At NikBearBrown, nikbearbrown dot com.
